root.narration: You receive a pass near the goal and a defender is closing in fast. You only have a second to get a shot off. The goalie is standing tall but there is a gap between his pads. What kind of shot do you take?

choices.text: Snap a quick wrist shot low through the gap between the goalie's pads
Wind up for a big slap shot aimed at the top corner
Try a slap shot but rush it and barely lift the stick off the ice

outcome.headline: What a snipe! Goal!
outcome.explanation: A wrist shot is quicker to release than a slap shot. Because the defender was closing in fast, the quick wrist shot found the gap before the goalie could squeeze his pads together.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you are close to the net and need a quick release, use a wrist shot. Save the big slap shot for when you have more time and space.

keyTerms.definition: A quick, accurate shot where you flick your wrists to push the puck off your stick blade. It is faster to release than a slap shot.

outcome.headline: Blocked! But good power
outcome.explanation: A slap shot is powerful, but the big windup took too long. The defender got their stick in the way and blocked your shot before it reached the net.
outcome.whatToRemember: Slap shots need time and space. When a defender is right on top of you, a quick wrist shot is usually the smarter choice.

outcome.headline: Whiff! The puck barely moved
outcome.explanation: A rushed slap shot where you do not make clean contact just dribbles the puck forward. The defender easily picked it up and cleared the zone.
outcome.whatToRemember: If you do not have time for a full windup, do not try a slap shot. A clean wrist shot is always better than a bad slap shot.
